title: Over US $121 billion in renewable investment at risk as increased federal oversight exposes 92 GW of projects to heightened permitting scrutiny
gist: A Wood Mackenzie analysis finds that a July 2025 DOI memorandum centralizing federal permitting review has already caused 7 GW of wind/solar project cancellations or inactivity on federal lands and now exposes 92 GW (US $121 billion) of the pipeline to heightened scrutiny, with wind proportionally hit hardest and solar hit hardest in absolute terms, though a April 2026 court injunction and the pending SPEED Act could ease the bottleneck.

insights.claim: A Department of Interior memorandum issued July 15, 2025 centralized federal review across nearly all wind and solar projects, introducing a multi-step approval process that lengthens permitting timelines for any project touching a federal agency.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: This single administrative memo is the mechanism driving tens of billions in at-risk renewable capacity — track DOI rule changes and litigation as a leading indicator for developer timelines and financing costs.
insights.quote: a Department of Interior (DOI) memorandum issued on July 15, 2025 centralized federal review across nearly all wind and solar projects, introducing a multi-step approval process that extends permitting timelines and increases scrutiny for any project involving a federal agency

insights.claim: Permitting changes and federal funding withdrawals directly caused 7 GW of renewable project cancellations or inactivity on federal lands in 2025, with an additional 12 GW on federal lands and 80 GW on private lands now newly exposed to heightened review, putting over $121 billion of investment at risk.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Investors in utility-scale wind/solar/storage developers should size exposure to federal-land or federally-adjacent projects, since $121B of pipeline value is directly tied to permitting-process risk rather than economics or technology.
insights.quote: heightened federal oversight could expose an additional 12 GW on federal lands and 80 GW on private lands, putting more than US $121 billion of investment at risk

insights.claim: 32% of the entire early-stage US renewable project pipeline (announced, under development, or already permitted) is now subject to additional federal review following the DOI rule change.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: A one-third pipeline exposure rate is a material systemic drag on US renewable buildout pace, relevant to utility capex planning and PPA supply forecasts.
insights.quote: Following the implementation of the DOI permitting rules, 32% of the early-stage project pipeline is now subject to additional federal review.

themes.theme: Uneven impact by technology and geography

insights.claim: Solar has the largest absolute pipeline exposure to permitting risk (30% of its pipeline), while wind has the highest proportional exposure at 62%, excluding the separate ongoing FAA halt; energy storage also faces over one-quarter of planned capacity under heightened scrutiny.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Wind developers face the steepest proportional regulatory drag (62% of pipeline), making wind-heavy portfolios more sensitive to permitting-policy outcomes than solar-heavy ones, even though solar carries more absolute dollars at risk.
insights.quote: solar has the largest absolute exposure, with 30% of its pipeline at risk of additional review. Wind, however, has the highest proportional exposure, with 62% of its pipeline affected (excluding the ongoing FAA halt)
insights.locator: Paragraph beginning 'The analysis indicates that solar has the largest absolute exposure'

insights.claim: Wetland-area permitting is the primary constraint across solar, wind and storage on private lands, with exposure concentrated in Oregon, Alabama, Maine, Minnesota and Montana, while wind projects are additionally constrained by airspace permitting.
insights.speaker: Kaitlin Fung, senior research analyst at Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Investors and developers can pre-screen project siting risk geographically: wetland-heavy states listed here carry elevated private-land permitting risk regardless of federal policy shifts, while wind-specific airspace rules are a separate risk factor to monitor near flight corridors.
insights.quote: permitting for wetland areas remain the primary constraint across solar, wind and energy storage. Wetlands account for the majority of private land exposure, with risk concentrated in Oregon, Alabama, Maine, Minnesota and Montana. However, wind projects are more constrained by airspace permitting.

insights.claim: Projects scheduled for 2029 hold the largest volume of at-risk capacity on federal lands, with exposure concentrated in Texas, California and Arizona, where delays could push commercial operation dates beyond plan and jeopardize tax credit eligibility.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Developers with 2029 commercial-operation targets in Texas, California or Arizona should treat permitting delay as a direct threat to tax credit eligibility, not just a schedule slip — a second-order financial risk beyond simple timeline extension.
insights.quote: Projects scheduled for 2029 account for the largest volume of capacity at risk of additional review on federal lands, which could jeopardize their tax credit eligibility. Exposure is highest in Texas, followed by California and Arizona

insights.claim: Not all recent renewable project cancellations are attributable to permitting challenges; some stem from supply chain constraints and tighter financing conditions instead.
insights.speaker: Kaitlin Fung, senior research analyst at Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Investors should disaggregate cancellation causes when assessing developer risk — permitting reform (e.g., SPEED Act) would not fully resolve cancellations driven by supply chain or financing issues.
insights.quote: it's important to note that not all cancellations are due to permitting challenges. Some also stem from supply chain constraints and tighter financing conditions

insights.claim: In April 2026 a federal court issued a preliminary injunction blocking agency actions that imposed new restrictions and expanded review requirements for wind and solar projects, ruling them likely unlawful under the Administrative Procedure Act, though the injunction does not resolve broader permitting bottlenecks.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: This injunction caps further deterioration in the near term but is not a full fix — investors should watch for appeal outcomes and whether the ruling is narrowed or overturned, since it currently only 'limits further disruption.'
insights.quote: a federal court issued a preliminary injunction blocking agency actions that imposed new restrictions and expanded review requirements for wind and solar projects, finding them likely unlawful under the Administrative Procedure Act

insights.claim: The SPEED Act, approved by the House in December 2025 and awaiting Senate and Executive approval, would narrow environmental review scope, reduce inter-agency duplication, and impose stricter permitting decision timelines, potentially meaningfully reducing permitting timelines for renewable and other infrastructure projects if enacted.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Senate/Executive action on the SPEED Act is a binary catalyst worth tracking — passage would materially de-risk the $121B pipeline currently exposed to permitting delay.
insights.quote: the Simplifying Permitting and Ending Endless Delays (SPEED) Act, approved by the House in December 2025 and currently awaiting Senate and Executive approval, proposes to narrow the scope of environmental reviews, reduce duplication across agencies and introduce stricter timelines for permitting decisions

insights.claim: Permitting uncertainty has a compounding effect on energy storage specifically because storage development is often tied to co-located solar projects, so delays in solar permitting cascade into storage delays.
insights.speaker: Gaby Ackermann Logan, Research Associate at Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Storage-focused investors should model permitting risk as correlated with, not independent from, solar permitting risk given the common co-location pattern — a solar permitting delay is effectively a storage delay too.
insights.quote: For storage in particular, where development is often tied to solar, permitting uncertainty has a compounding effect.
